$comment: Investigation findings from raw-data profiling and the post-ETL parquet output. A Power BI build skill should treat this as: (a) validation targets to verify after loading, (b) headline KPIs to feature on cards, (c) priors for top-N visuals. Generated 2026-04-28.

validationTargets.$comment: If a row count or distinct count doesn't match these after loading, something went wrong with the ingestion.

QUANTITY.$comment: Extreme outlier — bulk/weighted item. Cap at p99 for visuals.

headlineKpis.$comment: These are the numbers a viewer should see first when the dashboard opens. Use them to anchor card visuals.

yoy.$comment: Aggregate YoY left null intentionally — the story is in the per-household trend distribution below, not the headline number.

householdTrendDistribution.$comment: From dim_household.HH_Trend. The central narrative: more households grew than declined.

marketingExposure.$comment: Of households that received campaigns, 27.4% redeemed at least one coupon.

exposedVsUnexposedAvgSpend.$caveat: Exposed cohort spends 4.37x more — this is heavily confounded with selection bias, NOT a clean causal lift. Campaigns are targeted at active customers. The dashboard's marketing-impact page MUST surface this caveat or it will mislead.

honestDiffInDiffLift.$comment: The proper answer to Q5. Computed in agg_household_campaign_lift.parquet. Pre-window = 28 days before campaign START_DAY; post-window = 28 days from START_DAY. Restricted to campaigns with a complete post-window in the observation period.
honestDiffInDiffLift.$takeaway: Direct marketing produced essentially zero short-term spend lift. Lead the marketing page with this number, not the 4.37x ratio.

demographicCoverage.$comment: Apply HasDemographics = TRUE filter on every demographic visual.

MARITAL_STATUS_CODE.$note: A typically married/partnered, B single, U unknown — confirm with project owner.

spendByIncome.$comment: Avg total spend per surveyed household by income bracket. Notice the dip at 200-249K (n=5) — too small to feature.

200-249K.$caveat: Sample too small (n=5) — flag or hide.

topCommoditiesBySales.$flag: Investigate — sounds non-merchandise. May represent coupon redemption ledger entries.

categoryWinnersLosers.$comment: Pre-computed for the storyboard's 'Winners & Losers' page. Filter min Y1 spend $5K to suppress noise. Notice that the same commodities appear at the top of BOTH lists — the same big-volume categories grew for Growing HH and shrank for Declining HH. The story isn't 'category X is dying' — it's 'engaged vs disengaged households diverge across high-volume staples'.

campaignPortfolio.$comment: Campaign 24 ends DAY 719 but transactions stop at DAY 711. Filter Extends_Past_Observation = false on lift analyses.

promotionFootprint.$comment: Display and mailer values are nominal codes — never sum or average. The OnDisplay_AnyStore / InMailer_AnyStore booleans collapse them into a usable signal.

knownDataQualityIssues.issue: Discounts stored as negatives in source
knownDataQualityIssues.fix: ABS() applied in ETL; columns now positive in fact_transactions.

knownDataQualityIssues.recommendation: Cap at p99 or use median for chart axes.
Always filter HasDemographics = TRUE on demographic visuals.
Filter Extends_Past_Observation = FALSE on lift visuals.

knownDataQualityIssues.fix: Has_Real_Category = FALSE in dim_product; filter on category visuals.

knownDataQualityIssues.issue: Marketing exposed/unexposed cohorts have 4.37x raw spend ratio
knownDataQualityIssues.recommendation: Likely selection bias — show as a caveat, not as 'lift'. A pre/post within-household design is more honest.

knownDataQualityIssues.issue: 'COUPON/MISC ITEMS' is the #1 commodity by sales
knownDataQualityIssues.recommendation: May not be a true product category. Consider excluding from category analyses or labelling distinctly.
Hide or footnote in income visuals.

narrativeRecommendations: Lead Page 1 with the 1,459 vs 891 (Growing vs Declining) split — this is the headline.
On Page 2, treat 'top categories' as relative to the cohort (Growing vs Declining), not absolute. The same commodities appear in both lists — that's the actual insight.
On Page 3, only show income/age/composition with HasDemographics filter applied; hide demographic visuals when filter is removed.
On Page 4, show the exposed/unexposed comparison BUT visually warn about selection bias. Lead with the funnel (received → redeemed = 27%) rather than the spend ratio.
Reserve real-estate at the top of every page for a 'context band' showing: Active Households | HasDemographics filter state | Year filter state. The user needs to know what slice they're looking at.
